coredata.dc:title: Multiple-cores@shell clustered carbon dots/P25/rGO nanocomposite as robust visible-light photocatalyst for organic pollutant degradation and water disinfection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Wenjin
Mi, Jiaoyang
Fu, Yuqin
Cui, Donghui
Lü, Changli
coredata.dc:description: A novel photocatalyst was developed by combining TiO2 (P25), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and multi-core@shell clustered carbon dots (CDs) by one-step hydrothermal method for the high efficiency visible-light degradation of organic dyes and antibacterial application. The as-synthesized CDs were used as photosensitizers of P25 to effectively broaden its visible absorption region from 410 to 800 nm. Meanwhile, the effects of different contents of CDs and rGO on the photocatalytic performance were systematically studied, in which 2% CDs/0.5% rGO/P25 composite displayed the most efficient photocatalytic degradation for organic dyes and photocatalytic antibacterial performance under visible light irradiation. The corresponding degradation rate constant of 2% CDs/0.5% rGO/P25 for RhB was 29, 2.8 and 1.3 times more than those of P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/P25 composite, respectively. This unique multi-core@shell clustered CDs played a vital role in improving the photocatalytic activity of the CDs/rGO/P25 composite, increasing visible light absorption, and promoting electron-hole pairs separation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multi-core@shell clustered carbon dots
TiO2
rGO
Photocatalytic
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: BET surface areas and BJH pore size distributions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of CDs, rGO, P25, CDs/P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/rGO/P25.
TEM images of multiple cores@shell clustered CDs (a, b), P25 (c), 2%CDs/0.5%rGO/P25 composite (d, the inset shows the corresponding HRTEM).
N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms for (a) P25, (b) CDs/rGO/P25 composite, and the corresponding pore size distributions (inset).
XPS spectra of (a) the survey, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) Ti 2p h of P25 and CDs/rGO/P25.
Raman spectra of GO, rGO/P25, CDs/P25 and CDs/rGO/P25 composites.

(a) UV–Vis DRS spectra of CDs, P25, CDs/P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/rGO/P25; (b) VB-XPS spectra of P25, CDs/P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/rGO/P25; (c) Schematic band structure evolution; (d) Transient photocurrent response of P25, CDs/P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/rGO/P25.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of RhB with P25, CDs/P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/rGO/P25 under visible light irradiation; (b) The corresponding kinetic fitting using first order reaction function; (c) Photocatalytic degradation of different pollutants with CDs/rGO/P25 under visible light irradiation; (d) Reusability of CDs/rGO/P25 for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB.

Inactivation curves of E. coli (a-f) and S. aureus (g-i), the bacteria solution under visible light irradiation without any catalyst (a, g) and with 3 mg of CDs/rGO/P25 (b, h), rGO/P25 (c), CDs/P25 (d), and P25 (e); the bacteria solution with 3 mg of CDs/rGO/P25 in the dark (f, i).

Fluorescence images of live and dead E. coli cells (a-c) and S. aureus cells (d–e) treated for 1 h with 200 μg/mL CDs/rGO/P25 composite under visible light irradiation (a, d), in the dark (b, e), and light control (c, f).

SEM images of E. coli (a-d) and S. aureus (e-h), untreated (a, e); and treated without any catalyst under visible light irradiation for 1 h (b, f); with 200 μg/mL CDs/rGO/P25 composite in the dark for 1 h (c, g); and with 200 μg/mL CDs/rGO/P25 composite under light irradiation for 1 h (d, h).
(a) Photocatalytic activity of CDs/rGO/P25 with different scavenges for RhB degradation; (b) ESR spectra of the DMPO-•O2 −and DMPO-•OH recorded with the CDs/rGO/P25 composite.
Schematic preparation of multiple cores@shell clustered CDs/rGO/P25 composite.
Schematic proposed mechanism of photocatalytic dye degradation for CDs/rGO/P25 composite under visible light.

head.ce:title: Multiple-cores@shell clustered carbon dots/P25/rGO nanocomposite as robust visible-light photocatalyst for organic pollutant degradation and water disinfection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Jilin Agricultural University, Changchun 130118, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, Jilin Agricultural University, Changchun 130118, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We used citric acid (CA) and 5-amino-1,10-phenanthroline (Aphen) as raw materials to synthesize multi-core@shell clustered CDs by one-step hydrothermal method. After being modified by CDs, the CDs/rGO/P25 composite revealed obvious light absorption in the visible light region from 410 to 800 nm. The as-prepared ternary 2%CDs/0.5%rGO/P25 composite exhibited high efficient photocatalytic activity for degradation of organic pollutants and photocatalytic antibacterial effect under visible light irradiation.

A novel photocatalyst was developed by combining TiO2 (P25), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and multi-core@shell clustered carbon dots (CDs) by one-step hydrothermal method for the high efficiency visible-light degradation of organic dyes and antibacterial application. The as-synthesized CDs were used as photosensitizers of P25 to effectively broaden its visible absorption region from 410 to 800 nm. Meanwhile, the effects of different contents of CDs and rGO on the photocatalytic performance were systematically studied, in which 2% CDs/0.5% rGO/P25 composite displayed the most efficient photocatalytic degradation for organic dyes and photocatalytic antibacterial performance under visible light irradiation. The corresponding degradation rate constant of 2% CDs/0.5% rGO/P25 for RhB was 29, 2.8 and 1.3 times more than those of P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/P25 composite, respectively. This unique multi-core@shell clustered CDs played a vital role in improving the photocatalytic activity of the CDs/rGO/P25 composite, increasing visible light absorption, and promoting electron-hole pairs separation.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the control and treatment of environmental pollution has become one of the major problem facing and urgent to be solved in human society, particularly in water pollution treatment is the top priority of our times[1]. Among many environmental pollution treatment technologies, the heterogeneous photocatalytic reaction with semiconductor oxide as the catalyst is considered as an ideal method, which has great economic and social benefits and has a broad application prospect in three fields of sewage treatment, air purification and cleaning and sterilization [2]. Since 1972, Fujishima and Honda first used titanium dioxide (TiO2) as a photoelectrode to generate hydrogen by electrolysis of water under light illumination [3], TiO2 and its related materials have been studied most thoroughly, due to its simple production process, low cost, non-toxic, and good degradation effect on toxic and harmful substances and bacteria. And a highly dispersed gas phase nano-sized TiO2 (P25) produced by Evonik Degussa has been commercialized. Because of its strong oxidation ability and excellent hydrophilicity, TiO2 has bactericidal and cleaning effects, which makes it expand in the field of photocatalysis. However, TiO2 reacts only to ultraviolet light in sunlight, because of its relatively wide band gap (3.0–3.2 eV) and short photogenerated charge carriers lifetime, which limits the utilization of visible light photons. Therefore, how to expand the visible light utilization of TiO2 and extend photogenerated charge carriers lifetime are the focus of our research.

To solve the above problems, many methods have been exploited, such as morphology control [4–6], loading noble metals [7–10], doping other elements [11–14], and coupling with other semiconductors [15–19]. Recently, in order to enhance photocatalytic activity, the coupling of TiO2 materials with nonmetallic carbonaceous materials, for instance, carbon nanotubes [20,21], C60 [22,23], graphene oxide [24–28], and carbon dots [29] for promoting the transfer of photogenerated electron-holes has attracted much attention. In particular, TiO2/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) composite showed a significant increase in photocatalytic activity because of unique optical properties, large specific surface area, rapid charge carriers mobility, and high chemical stability of rGO [25]. The heterojunction can effectively diminish the band gap of TiO2, so that the resulting composite catalyst can absorb visible light, reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and promote the rapid photoelectron transfer from the conduction band of TiO2 to rGO [27]. Although it is improved for the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes and photocatalytic antibacterial properties of TiO2-rGO composite, but the low visible light utilization and the low oxidizing ability still restrict their wide applications in environmental management [30,31].

Carbon dots (CDs) are the youngest member of the family of carbon-based nanomaterials [32]. CDs have the unique optical properties of quantum dots and the remarkable electrical properties of sp2 hybrid, and are widely used in the field of photocatalysis [33]. To date, various methods of preparing CDs have been published, which can be categorized into two types: “bottom-up” and “top-down” [34]. In recent years, many applications of CDs/TiO2 composite in photocatalysis area have been reported, e.g., nitrogen-doped carbon dots/TiO2 [35], TiO2-graphene quantum dot [36], CQDs/TiO2 nanotubes [37], CQDs/TiO2 nanosheet [38], meso-TiO2/CDs [39], and CQDs-TiO2−x/rGO [40]. However, most of these reported carbon dots for photocatalysis are thermally synthesized from citric acid and ethylenediamine or urea, or obtained by electrolysis of graphite rods. The as-synthesized GQDs/TiO2 composite showed much enhanced photocatalytic activity in the degradation of RhB and MB as compared to the bare TiO2 under the UV light irradiation [41,42]. The CDs possess abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface with the characteristic of up-conversion. However, the most CDs absorb long-wavelength light and convert them into short-wavelength light, thereby exciting TiO2, while TiO2 only responds to ultraviolet light (λ ≤ 400 nm), so the light absorption for the reported composites of CDs-TiO2 is very limited. Various CDs are widely synthesized in order to enhance the visible light absorption of the catalyst. Yu et al. presented a facile and green route to cleanly and efficiently utilize low-rank lignite to produce carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in high yields and the lignite-derived CQDs exhibited unusual aggregation-induced different color emissions and strong orange-red solid-state fluorescence, and can effectively narrow the bandgap of TiO2 [43].

Herein, we used citric acid (CA) and 5-amino-1,10-phenanthroline (Aphen) as raw materials to synthesize multi-core@shell clustered CDs by one-step hydrothermal method. The constructed CDs have a very wide light absorption range (200–800 nm) and strong capability of coordination, which can evidently improve visible light absorption and photo-generated charge carries separation of P25. We successfully constructed multi-core@shell clustered CDs/rGO/P25 composite through hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Then the application of the nanohybrid composites in photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes and photocatalytic antibacterial activities was systematically studied. Furthermore, the possible photocatalytic mechanism of the CDs/rGO/P25 composite was studied by free radical capture experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis.

P25 was obtained from Degussa Co. Ltd., Germany. Citric acid (CA, 99.5%), rhodamine B (RhB, AR), methyl orange (MO, 96%), methylene blue (MB, AR), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 98%), and p-benzoquinone (BQ, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin. Isopropanol (IPA) was provided by Fuchen (Tianjin) chemical reagents Co., Ltd. 5-Amino-1,10-phenanthroline (Aphen, 97%) and 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

CDs were synthesized from CA and Aphen as raw materials through an typical hydrothermal route. CA (1.35 g) and Aphen (0.15 g) were added into deionized water (15 mL), and the mixture was dissolved by ultrasound. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon sealed autoclave (25 mL) and then heated to 200 °C for 7 h. After the reaction autoclave is cooled to ambient temperature, the reaction solution was centrifuged to remove large particles. Then, the unreacted small molecules were removed by dialysis for 72 h (3500 Da). The brown solid CDs were obtained by freeze-drying under vacuum.

0.2 g P25 was dispersed in 20 mL deionized water. 1.0 mL GO aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) was added to the P25 solution under magnetic stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Different amounts of CD’s aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) was dripped into above solution, and the reaction was stirred for 12 h. And then the mixed solution was treated at 120 °C for 3 h with a Teflon sealed autoclave (50 mL). Finally, the CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposites with different amounts of CDs (0, 1, 2, and 3 wt%) were obtained by washing with deionized water and absolute ethanol, and the products were dried overnight at 60 °C. As a contrast, different amounts of GO (0, 0.1, 0.5 and 1 wt%) were added when the content of CDs was 2 wt%.

The morphologies, and crystal structures of as-prepared samples were obtained through the JEM-2100F electron microscope (TEM) and the Bruker D8 (Cu Ka radiation) advance diffractometer (XRD). The FTIR spectra of as-prepared samples were obtained from a FTIR spectrometer (Magna 560) with KBr as the blank sample. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were examined by a Micromeritics ASAP 2020M N2 adsorption apparatus. The energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) analysis was studied by Hitachi SU8010. The surface chemical states and the element content of samples were examined using a ULVAC-PHI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). Raman spectra were taken using a Micro-Raman spectrometer system with an excitation wavelength of 488 nm. PL spectra were recorded on a Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer, and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a SHIMADZU UV-2550 UV–visible spectrophotometer. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of photocatalysts were analyzed by using a CARY500Scan UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer. The observation for cell morphology was performed on a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi SU8010). Electron spin resonance (ESR) signals could be obtained by Bruker A300-10/12. Cell membrane integrity was observed by fluorescence microscopy (Zeiss Axiovert A1).

Photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectral (EIS) measurements were investigated using a electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D), with a standard three-electrode cell, in which using Pt wire and Ag/AgCl served as a counter electrode and a reference electrode, respectively. Photocurrent was measured under 300 W xenon lamp as the light source. After 2 mg as-prepared sample was dispersed into 1 mL water by ultrasonic, the obtained stable suspension was dip-coated on ITO glass as the working photoelectrode and using 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte. EIS measurements were performed with a bias voltage of 0.5 V open circuit potential, these experiments were implemented in 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] containing KCl (0.1 M) solution. The ethanol solution of photocatalyst (10 mg/mL) was dripped on the glass carbon electrodes as working electrode.

The photocatalytic degradation tests were carried out by adding 50 mg of photocatalysts to a reactor containing 50 mL of aqueous solution of organic pollutants (10 ppm). A 300 W Xenon lamp serves as a visible light source with a cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm), and the reaction temperature was maintained at ambient temperature by circulating condensed water. The reaction system was sonicated for 10 min before irradiation to ensure that the photocatalyst was evenly dispersed, and then magnetically stirred for 30 min in the dark. During the reaction, 3 mL solution was taken out at regular intervals, the catalyst in the solution was removed by centrifugation, and then the concentration of residual pollution in solution was quantified by measuring the intensity of absorption peak at λmax = 553 nm (Rhodamine B), 664 nm (methylene blue), and 462 nm (methyl orange) with an UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV-2550).

Gram-negative E. coli and Grampositive S. aureus were applied to assess the photocatalytic antibacterial activity of samples. A certain amount of bacteria were added to the nutrient broth and shaken overnight at 37 °C to produce a cell count of about 108 colony forming units (CFU)/mL. The cells were centrifuged for 6 min at 6000 rpm to remove the broth medium, washed twice with the sterile phosphate buffer solution (PBS), and then diluted to 103 CFU/mL. The 30 mL E. coli solution was mixed with 3 mg photocatalyst in the dark for 10 min using a visible light source (300 W xenon lamp with a 420 nm filter), and the mixed solution of the bacteria and the photocatalyst was irradiated for 1 h. 0.1 mL suspension was taken out every 15 min for uniform coating on the LB agar plates (undiluted, two replicates), that was incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The bacterial concentration was determined by standard plate counting. The photocatalytic antibacterial experiments of S. aureus were carried out under the same conditions. Control experiments included light without catalyst, catalyst without light, and a series of comparative catalysts for antimicrobial activity.

SEM was used to analyze the morphology of E. coli and S. aureus. 2.0 mg photocatalys was added to 10 mL bacterial suspension (108 CFU/ml), then the solution was irradiated for 60 min. 1 mL suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the solution, and was treated with 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution (1 mL) at 4 °C for 12 h. The bacteria were washed twice with deionized water, dehydrated successively with 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, and 100% ethanol for 15 min and then washed with deionized water again 3 times. Finally, the solution of bacteria was dripped onto the silicon wafer. In the control experiment, there was cell suspension without any treatment, only the light radiation without any catalyst and the dark condition contained 2.0 mg catalyst. Finally, the morphology observation of E. coli and S. aureus were tested with SEM (Hitachi SU8010).

Death of E. coli and S. aureus were analyzed by fluorescence cell live/dead test. The cells were collected by centrifugation of 1 mL of the treated cell suspension in 2.7. According to the kit instructions, the cells were stained with PI and DAPI, then were observed by fluorescence microscopy (Zeiss Axiovert A1). The cell suspension with photocatalyst in dark and under visible light irradiated without photocatalyst were taken as the control experiment.

ESR signals of superoxide radical (•O2 −), hydroxyl radical (•OH) were obtained by Bruker A300-10/12. The 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as the spin-trap reagent and a 300 W Xe lamp was utilized as the visible light source with a cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm).

Reactive species (RSs) trapping experiments were carried out to confirm the roles of RSs in the photodegradation of RhB. We used a method similar to the above photocatalytic degradation experiments, where 1.0 mM BQ, 10 mM IPA and 10 mM EDTA-2Na were used as scavengers for superoxide radical (•O2 −), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and photogenerated hole (h+), respectively.

XRD was utilized to analyze the crystal structure of the synthesized photocatalyst samples. The XRD patterns of different samples are shown in Fig. 1 . Diffraction pattern of P25 shows the major peaks around the 2θ values of 25.3 and 27.5 corresponding to anatase (101) and rutile (110) phases respectively, confirming the biphasic nature of the P25. According to Zhang’s [44] calculation method, the weight fraction of rutile phase appeared in P25 (WR) is about 19.4%. In detail, the calculation is WR = AR/(0.884AA + AR), where AA and AR represent intensities of the strongest (101) peak of anatase and (110) peak of rutile, respectively. For the rGO and CDs, a broad band at about 2θ = 24.5° can be observed due to the existence of π − π stacking. The characteristic peaks of the CDs/rGO/P25 composite are similar to that of P25 nanoparticles, indicating the loading of the CDs and rGO did not change the crystal phase of the P25. However, CDs/rGO/P25 had a wide peak at 2θ = 24.5°, indicating an intense interface interaction between the different components of CDs, rGO, and P25.

TEM was utilized to observe the morphology of the synthesized photocatalyst samples. The TEM images of CDs prepared from CA and Aphen showed an about 100 nm multi-core@shell clustered dot morphology with a shell thickness of 10 nm (Fig. 2 a and b). The possible formation mechanism of the multiple core@shell-structured CDs has been proposed in the previous work from our group[45]. Fig. 2c shows the TEM image of P25 with the corresponding average size of 25 nm. After the coupling of the CDs, GO, and P25, the TEM of CDs/rGO/P25 composite shows a flower-like morphology with the uniform size of 90–100 nm. The inset in Fig. 2d displays a lattice spacing of 0.35 nm which is corresponding to the (101) plane of P25. After hydrothermal treatment, P25 particles were deposited onto the surface of CDs accompanied by the carbonization and size-shrinkage of aggregates. EDS spectrum of CDs/rGO/P25 composite is given in Fig. 3 . In this spectrum, Ti, C, O, N and Si elements are detectable. The sample was dripped onto the silicon wafer, so Si element is detectable. The other peaks are not shown in this figure, so the purity and composition of the product were further confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BET surface areas and BJH pore size distributions
ce:section.ce:para: The BET surface area and pore-size distribution of the as-prepared catalysts were measured by the N2 adsorption–desorption method. As shown in Fig. 4 . The P25 and CDs/rGO/P25 composite exhibited typical type-IV adsorption isotherms, indicating that the as-prepared catalysts belong to mesoporous materials. The BET surface area of P25 was 47.04 m2/g, and it was reduced to 46.73 m2/g after P25 was modified by rGO and CDs, which is mainly attributed to the introduction of CDs that cause the individual particles be tightly connected by chemical bonds. Moreover, the average pore diameter and pore volume did not exhibit significant changes in P25 and CDs/rGO/P25. All these findings confirmed the observations and discussion from the TEM analysis.

UV − vis absorption spectra (Fig. S1) clearly showed the as-synthesized CDs have different absorption bands with a strong, broad absorption of sunlight in the ultraviolet and visible regions. The as-prepared CDs in ethanol solution exhibited interesting PL properties with the change of excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength is below 400 nm, the CDs mainly displayed a strong excitation-wavelength-independent blue emission at 430 nm and a weak red emission peak at 630 nm. The FTIR spectra of CDs, rGO, P25, rGO/P25, CDs/P25, and CDs/rGO/P25 are shown in Fig. S2. The broad bands, which can be related to the bending and stretching vibrational modes of Ti-O-Ti bonds, were observed in the range of 500–800 cm−1 for P25. The peaks at 3066, 1570, 1401, and 1168 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of C-H, C=O, C=C, and C-N, respectively [46]. The peak at 1720 cm−1 corresponds to the amide bond (CONH) due to the amidation reaction between CA and Aphen [45]. For the CDs/rGO/P25, several characteristic absorption peaks of CDs were observed and the peaks at 1704, 1582, 1395, and 1168 cm−1 correspond to the CONH, C=O, C=C, and C–N bonds, respectively. Meanwhile, the characteristic peak of rGO at 1159 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration for C–O–C bond in CDs/rGO/P25 composite. So FTIR spectra of the as-prepared CDs/rGO/P25 composite further confirms an intense interface interaction between the different components of CDs, rGO, and P25.

The chemical composition, element content, and functional groups of CDs were investigated by XPS. As displayed in Fig. S3, there are three different peaks, namely C 1s (285.09 eV), O 1s (531.05 eV), and N 1s (400.55 eV). The atomic contents of C/O/N in elemental analysis of CDs are 70.39, 27.83, and 1.78%, respectively. The chemical composition of P25 and CDs/rGO/P25 composite were characterized by XPS. Fig. 5 a displays the XPS survey spectra of P25 and CDs/rGO/P25 composite. CDs/rGO/P25 composite shows the increasing peak intensity of C element, identifying the existence of CDs and rGO in this composite. Fig. 5b shows the high resolution C 1s spectra of P25 and CDs/rGO/P25 composite. The new fitted peaks located at 284.50 eV isattributed to the Ti-O-C bond of the CDs/rGO/P25 composite, and the other peaks at 284.83, 285.95 and 288.64 eV are attributed to the background carbon, C–O and C=O, respectively [47]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the peaks at 529.54 and 531.70 eV in the high-resolution O 1s spectra are related to the lattice oxygen and Ti-OH bonds of P25 [48]. For the CDs/rGO/P25 composite, there is a new fitted peak located at 533.33 eV corresponding to hydroxyl functional group (C-OH), which further proofs the chemical connections between CDs and P25 [40]. The corresponding Ti 2p spectra (Fig. 5d) shows two peaks at 458.27 and 464.04 eV ascribed to the binding energies of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 [49].

Raman spectra of GO, CDs/P25, rGO/P25 and CDs/rGO/P25 are shown in Fig. 6 . Graphene oxide showed two peaks at ~1361 and ~1601 cm−1 corresponds to D- and G- bands, respectively. In the Raman spectrum for GO the intensity of D band is lower than that of G band clearly, and the peak intensity ratio ID/IG for GO was found to be 0.92. The peak intensity ratio ID/IG for rGO/P25 composite was increased to 1.02, which proved the reduction of GO to rGO during hydrothermal process. However, The peak intensity ratio ID/IG for CDs/rGO/P25 composite was found to be 0.98, meaning that CDs may hinder the reduction of some GO sheets.

One of the key factors affecting the photocatalytic performance is the photoabsorption of photocatalysts. UV–Vis DRS was utilized to explore the light absorption of the as-obtained photocatalysts. As displayed in Fig. 7 a, pure P25 has a light absorption range lower than 410 nm. When P25 was modified by rGO, the light absorption range was extended to 423 nm. However, after the co-modification of rGO and CDs, the CDs/rGO/P25 composite revealed obvious visible light absorption from 410 to 800 nm. These results provide that CDs play a key role in improving visible light activity of photocatalyst. In addition, according to the empirical formula (Eg = 1240/λg), the band gap energy (Eg) for the P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/rGO/P25 were estimated to be 3.07, 2.87 and 2.65 eV, respectively [50]. Furthermore, in order to research the band gap of the photocatalysts, X-ray photoelectron spectra (Fig. 7b) was used to analyze the valence band (VB) potentials of the P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/rGO/P25 composite. Using the equation (ECB = EVB - Eg), the conduction band (CB) potentials of the samples were calculated. The schematic diagrams of the energy band structure of different materials are shown in Fig. 7c. Evidently, due to the co-modification of rGO and CDs, the band gap of the CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite is significantly narrowed, which leads to the more efficient visible-light absorption and improves the photocatalytic activity.

According to the PEC measurements, the excitation and transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes in the photocatalysts were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 7d, it is evident that all samples exhibited steady and repeatable photocurrent responses on each light illumination. The photocurrent signals increased quickly once the light was on, and the photocurrent rapidly decreased to zero when the light source was turned off. The CDs/rGO/P25 composite exhibits the highest photocurrent intensity, indicating that the CDs/rGO/P25 composite has the longest excition lifetime and the most efficient photo-generated carrier separation. The result reflects that the combination of P25 with CDs and GO can significantly improve the photoelectric response and charge separation. Moreover, the charge transfer resistance can be detected by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in dark and it is proportional to the diameter of the semicircular Nyquist plot. From Fig. S4, compared with bare P25, the CDs/P25 composite displays a smaller arc radius, reflecting a smaller charge transfer resistance in these heterostructured nanocomposites. After loading the rGO, the arc radiuses of rGO/P25 and CDs/rGO/P25 are remarkably decreased, suggesting the rGO can enhance the electrical conductivity of the samples. Among these as-prepared samples, the CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite shows the most efficient charge transfer efficiency and the photo-generated carrier separation in the photocatalysis process.

The photocatalytic activity of as-synthesized materials was studied through the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes and photocatalytic antibacterial activity under visible light irradiation. Fig. 8 a shows the photocatalytic degradation activity of RhB solution under visible light irradiation, using P25, CDs/P25, rGO/P25, and CDs/rGO/P25 composites as photocatalysts. The photocatalysts were added to the RhB solution and stirred in dark condition for 30 min to obtain the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Compared with P25 and CDs/P25 composite, the rGO/P25 and CDs/rGO/P25 composite exhibited higher adsorption capacities owing to the introduction of rGO with strong adsorption capacity. In addition, 98.6% of RhB was degraded within 45 min in the presence of CDs/rGO/P25 composite, and nearly 100% of RhB was removed completely within 60 min (Fig. 8a). The photocatalytic reaction kinetics are fitted with a pseudo-first-order reaction mode on account of the very low concentration of RhB (10 mg/L) [51]. The values of the apparent rate constants (k) were calculated utilizing the RhB degradation curves of -ln(C/C0) vs light irradiation time (t) (Fig. 8b). The CDs/rGO/P25 composite shows the highest k value (0.087 min−1), followed by CDs/P25 (0.068 min−1), rGO/P25 (0.031 min−1), and P25 (0.003 min−1). From the above results, it can be seen that the narrower the band gap of the composite, the higher the photocatalytic activity. Due to the narrower the band gap of the composite, the more photons can be effectively utilized in the visible light region, resulting in more photogenerated electron-hole pairs which were produced in the nanocomposite, so the nanocomposite exhibited higher photocatalytic performance. Besides, the as-prepared CDs/rGO/P25 photocatalyst in our work exhibits higher photocatalytic activity as compared to previously reported similar photocatalysts with CDs and TiO2 (Table S1). This is mainly attributed to the wide light absorption range of multi-core@shell clustered CDs and the good combination with TiO2, which provides efficient electron-hole pair separation. To further explore the photocatalytic activity of the modified P25 with both CDs and rGO, the effect of loading amounts of CDs and rGO was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, 2% CDs and 0.5% rGO are optimum content for the synthesis of CDs/rGO/P25 photocatalyst. Therefore, all the ternary CDs/rGO/P25 composite catalysts in this work refer to the optimal ratio. The CDs/rGO/P25 composite was further applied at the degradation of different cationic and anionic dyes under the same conditions, such as RhB, MB, and MO. As given in Fig. 8c, compared to anionic dye (MO), the CDs/rGO/P25 composite has higher adsorption performance for cationic dyes (RhB, MB), which is attributed to rGO with a negative charge. Meanwhile, under visible-light irradiation, the RhB and MB removal efficiency is about 100%, and the degradation efficiency of MO can also reaches 98% in 60 min. In addition, for a photocatalyst, the durability and stability are significantly important to practical applications. Thus, the cycle experiment for the photodegradation of RhB was carried out to demonstrate the stability of the CDs/rGO/P25 photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 8d, following five cycles, no significant deactivation was observed. The XRD patterns of CDs/rGO/P25 composite before and after five cycles had no obvious change in characteristic peaks (Fig. S6). The result proves that the CDs/rGO/P25 composite has good stability.

To well explore the photocatalytic potential of CDs/rGO/P25 composite, we also performed the photocatalytic bacterial inactivation experiments. The E. coli (Gram negative) and S. aureus (Gram positive) served as the model bacteria to assess the antibacterial capability of the samples. Fig. S7 and S8 show the visualized E. coli and S. aureus bacterial viability on LB-agar plates after treated with CDs/rGO/P25 composite under visible light irradiation and in the dark. Meanwhile, a controlled experiment was performed without photocatalyst under visible light irradiation. The results showed that CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite had obvious antibacterial activity under visible light irradiation. Meanwhile, using E. coli as the model, the photocatalytic antibacterial effect of the control samples under visible light irradiation was studied as given in Fig. S9. The antibacterial effect can also be evaluated by the relation curves of bacterial survival rate (N/N0) and time (Fig. 9 ). The pure P25, CDs/P25, rGO/P25 and CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposites showed the antibacterial efficacy of 15.03, 75.63, 64.05 and 100% against E. coli with 60 min, respectively. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 9b, 100% of S. aureus was inactivated within 45 min, proving that CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite has high efficiency photocatalytic antibacterial activity, and this is significant for controlling microbial pollution.

To further verify the photocatalytic antibacterial activity of CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite, cell live/dead fluorescent test was conducted utilizing fluorescence microscopy imaging method. Bacterial suspensions of E. coli and S. aureus were treated for 1 h with 200 μg/mL CDs/rGO/P25 composite under visible light irradiation, then bacteria were stained by DAPI and PI fluorescence dye reagents. The cell live/dead was determined by fluorescence microscopy. It is well known that DAPI is cell-permeable blue-fluorescent colouring agent, and PI is a cell-permeable red-fluorescent colouring agent. The DAPI reagent is applied to label all cells, because it can combine with DNA in the nucleus without being affected by the cell membrane. However, the PI reagent can only combine with DNA in the broken membrane. So PI reagent is often applied to mark dead cells. As shown in Fig. 10 a and d, in the light control experiment, only a few dead cells appeared at fluorescence images, which demonstrates that visible light cannot damage the membrane proteins of cells. For the E. coli and S. aureus were treated with 200 μg/mL CDs/rGO/P25 composite in the dark or under visible light irradiation for 60 min, respectively, as shown in Fig. 10b and e, in dark conditions, very few bacteria cells were killed, indicating that CDs/rGO/P25 composite itself has poor disinfection properties. However, as shown in Fig. 10c and f, owing to the cell membrane breakage, almost all bacteria cells were stained with PI compared to cells stained with DAPI, demonstrating that CDs/rGO/P25 composite has excellent photocatalytic antibacterial activity under visible light irradiation.

Furthermore, we investigated the antibacterial mechanism and bacterial morphological changes of the E. coli and S. aureus by SEM characterization. As shown in Fig. 11 a and e, the untreated E. coli and S. aureus retained their connatural morphology and an intact cell structure. There are also no obvious morphological changes of E. coli and S. aureus in the light control experiment and were treated with CDs/rGO/P25 composite in the dark (Fig. 11b, c, f, and g). However, as shown in Fig. 11d and h, after treatment with 200 μg/mL CDs/rGO/P25 composite under visible light irradiation for 60 min, the shape of the cells are seriously distorted and the membranes of cells are badly damaged. Therefore, the excellent antimicrobial property of CDs/rGO/P25 composite is mainly derived from the destruction membranes of cells.

To understand the possible photocatalytic mechanism of the degradation of organic dyes and inactivation of bacteria, some additional experiments were also carried out. The major active species in the photocatalysis reaction were studied though reactive species trapping tests, such as trapped h+, •OH radicals, and •O2 − radicals related to the photocatalysis process, and the results are shown in Fig. 12 a. In these experiments, three different radical scavengers include EDTA-2Na (h+ scavengers), IPA (•OH scavengers), and BQ (•O2 − scavengers) were added into the photocatalytic degradation system of RhB, respectively. When IPA was added, the degradation of RhB was not significantly inhibited, which indicated that •OH radicals are not the dominant active species during the photocatalytic reaction. However, when BQ and EDTA-2Na radical sacrificial agents were present in the reaction system, the degradation times were extended and the degradation rates decreased substantially to 34.9% and 41.4%, respectively. It means that •O2 − and h+ were considered as the dominant active species in the photocatalytic degradation reaction.

Meanwhile, the photocatalytic mechanism was further investigated in more detail. The ESR spin-trap signals of CDs/rGO/P25 composite were detected using DMPO as the spin-trapping agent. Fig. 12b showed that there no ESR signals were observed in the dark. However, the characteristic peaks of the DMPO-•O2 − and DMPO-•OH species could be found under visible light irradiation, which indicated the generation of •OH and •O2 − radicals during the reaction system. Moreover, the signals of •O2 − are stronger than that of •OH, implying that •O2 − radicals probably have more important influence on the photocatalytic process than •OH radicals. In short, these experimental results showed that the influence order of active species was •O2 − > h+ >•OH in the photocatalytic process of CDs/rGO/P25 composite.

According to the above experimental results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed to describe photocatalytic degradation and bactericidal mechanism of CDs/rGO/P25 composite as illustrated in Scheme 2 . The high photocatalytic activities of CDs/rGO/P25 composite can be attributed to increased absorption of visible light and efficient separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs. Owing to the unique optical properties and rapid charge carriers mobility, rGO can be used as electron acceptor to enhance the electron-hole pair separation. Meanwhile, when P25 was modified by rGO, the band gap of the rGO/P25 composite was significantly reduced to 2.87 eV, and its light absorption edge was extended to 432 nm. When rGO/P25 composite was modified by CDs, the optical absorption range of CDs/rGO/P25 composite was further prolonged to 468.54 nm, which should be mainly due to the wide absorption range and strong coordination ability of CDs. Thus, under visible light irradiation, the CDs/rGO/P25 composite produced a lot of electrons and holes, then the electrons (e-) could readily combine with dissolved oxygen in water to form •O2 −, whereas some photogenerated holes (h+) could combine with H2O/OH− to produce •OH. The produced active species (•O2 −, h+, and •OH) can effectively degrade organic dyes and attack common pathogenic bacteria. Consequently, high efficiency photocatalytic activity was attributed to the synergistic effect of the CDs/rGO/P25 composite.

In general, the novel CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite was constructed successfully via combining rGO, P25 and multi-core@shell clustered CDs for the first time. The experimental results showed that the as-prepared ternary 2%CDs/0.5%rGO/P25 composite with high stability exhibited high efficient degradation for organic dyes and antibacterial properties under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic activity was markedly improved mainly due to the synergistic effect of multi-core@shell clustered CDs combined with P25 and rGO, and the high efficient the separation of electron-hole pairs. In brief, our work proves that the multi-core@shell clustered CDs can significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity of P25, and it is expected to be applied in the more extensive photocatalytic fields to face the increasing energy demand and environmental problems.

Wenjin Zhu: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Jiaoyang Mi: Investigation, Resources. Yuqin Fu: Conceptualization, Methodology. Donghui Cui: Data curation, Validation, Software. Changli Lü: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.148087.
